statusLine: European fuel security remains under strain with 19 of 27 countries holding critical reserves in at least one fuel type, despite EU-wide averages above mandatory minimums.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel stocks show concerning disparities across member states, with 19 of 27 countries maintaining critical reserves in at least one fuel category despite EU averages remaining above the 90-day mandatory minimum. Romania faces the most severe shortages with all three fuel types in critical status - petrol at just 33.2 days, diesel at 32.4 days, and jet fuel at 76.4 days. Poland, Luxembourg, and Germany also show critical levels across all categories, indicating widespread pressure on major European economies.

Jet fuel emerges as the most vulnerable fuel type EU-wide, averaging 91.9 days compared to 98.9 days for petrol and 97.8 days for diesel. A striking 20 of 27 countries hold critical jet fuel reserves, potentially impacting aviation connectivity. This pattern suggests aviation fuel supply chains face particular strain from recent Middle East disruptions and strategic reserve releases.

Oil prices reflect ongoing supply tensions, with Brent crude at $105.33 per barrel - up 45.3% since late February escalations began. European consumers face significant price variations, with Netherlands paying €2.279 per liter for petrol while Malta enjoys €1.34 per liter. These price disparities, combined with uneven stock distributions, highlight the fragmented nature of European energy security as geopolitical pressures persist.
keyPoints: 19 of 27 EU countries maintain critical fuel reserves in at least one category, with Romania showing the lowest levels across all fuel types
Jet fuel stocks are most vulnerable with 20 countries in critical status, despite the EU average of 91.9 days exceeding mandatory minimums
Brent crude has surged 45.3% since late-February Middle East escalations, reaching $105.33 per barrel
Major economies including Germany, France, Italy, and Poland all show critical reserves in multiple fuel categories
Fuel price variations across the EU remain extreme, with Netherlands petrol costs 70% higher than Malta's €1.34 per liter
